talk_title: Revisiting the neoantigen approach to cancer immunotherapy

biomarkers.name: HLA loss of heterozygosity
biomarkers.finding: proposed as a biomarker to prioritize early multi‑neoantigen targeting

biomarkers.name: MSI status
biomarkers.finding: low TYW2 expression correlates with better response to checkpoint blockade

models.name: immunocompetent C57BL/6 mice bearing TYW2‑KO melanoma

MOA_summary: Samuels demonstrated that inhibiting translation fidelity via TYW2 knockout induces programmed ribosomal frameshifts, generating abundant out‑of‑frame peptides that are presented on MHC and elicit strong CD8‑T‑cell responses; chemotherapy can further boost neoantigen presentation, and recurrent driver‑derived neoantigens can be identified through HLA‑matched population databases for vaccine or TCR‑based therapies.
key_findings: Chemotherapy (lymphodepleting) synergizes with KRAS G12V‑specific TCRs by increasing neoantigen presentation.
TYW2 knockout in melanoma cells produces a programmed increase in out‑of‑frame peptides, leading to CD8‑dependent tumor rejection in mice.
Ribosome profiling revealed phenylalanine‑codon pausing and both –1 and +1 frameshifts in TYW2‑KO cells.
Identified >350,000 patients per year with recurrently presented neoantigens using a data‑driven HLA‑matched pipeline.
Bacterial‑derived peptides and translation‑infidelity peptides expand the antigenic repertoire beyond somatic mutations.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Neoantigens derived from passenger mutations are subclonal and prone to immunoevasion.
Low‑TMB tumors lack sufficient neoantigens, limiting vaccine efficacy.

combination_strategies.rationale: enhances MHC presentation of targeted neoantigens
combination_strategies.phenotype: synergistic tumor regression in mouse models

combination_strategies.rationale: releases CD8 T‑cell activity against newly presented frameshift peptides
combination_strategies.phenotype: complete tumor rejection in TYW2‑KO mice

open_questions: How does HLA loss of heterozygosity impact the durability of multi‑neoantigen targeting?
Can RNA‑methylation pathways be exploited to further increase translation‑infidelity neoantigens?

talk_title: Pharmacologic inhibition of PMS2 to induce mismatch repair deficiency

targets.dependency_context: colorectal and breast cancer cell lines

biomarkers.name: MSI score
biomarkers.finding: dose‑dependent increase to MSI‑high levels in treated cells

biomarkers.name: Tumor mutational burden
biomarkers.finding: gradual rise in mutations/Mb correlating with 1867 concentration

clinical_data.value: prolonged in a subset of patients
clinical_data.population: patients with increased TMB after temozolomide

MOA_summary: The small‑molecule 1867 covalently binds cysteine‑73 in the ATPase domain of PMS2, selectively disabling its mismatch‑repair activity; treated cells acquire MSI‑high status, high mutational burden, and become sensitive to PD‑1 blockade, recapitulating the phenotype of genetic PMS2 loss without cytotoxicity.
key_findings: 1867 selectively engages PMS2 cysteine‑73 with >99% specificity among >20,000 cysteines.
Pharmacologic PMS2 inhibition phenocopies genetic knockout, abolishing 6‑guanine‑induced DNA nicks.
Dose‑dependent induction of MSI‑high and mutational signatures (SBS21, SBS15) in vitro.
1867‑treated tumor cells reject in immunocompetent mice when combined with anti‑PD‑1, but not in immunodeficient hosts.
Combination of 1867 with FOLFOXIRI accelerates MSI conversion and can induce tumor regression without checkpoint blockade.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Potential redundancy among MMR proteins may limit efficacy of PMS2 inhibition alone.
Toxicity concerns addressed by non‑cytotoxic profile of 1867 in normal cells.

combination_strategies.rationale: checkpoint blockade unleashes CD8 T‑cell response against newly generated neoantigens
combination_strategies.phenotype: complete tumor regression in mouse models

combination_strategies.rationale: chemotherapy accelerates acquisition of MSI/high TMB when paired with PMS2 inhibition
combination_strategies.phenotype: rapid MSI conversion and tumor rejection without additional checkpoint therapy

open_questions: Can oral formulations of 1867 achieve sufficient tumor exposure in patients?
Will simultaneous inhibition of multiple MMR components improve conversion speed?

talk_title: Chemically inducing mismatch repair deficiency to sensitize tumors to PD‑1 blockade

targets.dependency_context: MSS cell lines treated with temozolomide+cisplatin
MSS cell lines treated with temozolomide+cisplatin

biomarkers.name: MSI score
biomarkers.finding: combined temozolomide+cisplatin raises MSI to high levels in treated cells

biomarkers.name: Tumor mutational burden
biomarkers.finding: increase of ~125 mutations/Mb after 8‑week combo treatment

biomarkers.name: MSH2 promoter methylation
biomarkers.finding: combo treatment induces MSH2 promoter methylation correlating with MSI rise

models.name: immunocompetent mice bearing MSS tumors
models.genotype_or_key_features: in vivo temozolomide+cisplatin followed by anti‑PD‑1

clinical_data.value: median not reached in MSI‑converted subgroup

MOA_summary: Temozolomide induces DNA methylation and MSH2 promoter silencing, while cisplatin adds DNA cross‑links; together they rapidly down‑regulate MSH2, generate a mismatch‑repair‑deficient mutational signature rich in frameshift indels, elevate TMB and MSI, and render tumors highly sensitive to PD‑1 blockade in preclinical models and a subset of patients.
key_findings: Temozolomide alone raises TMB but not MSI; cisplatin alone modestly increases frameshifts.
The combination yields a dramatic TMB gain (~125 mut/Mb) and high MSI scores with predominantly subclonal frameshift mutations.
MSH2 promoter methylation drives loss of MSH2 expression after 4–8 weeks of combo treatment.
Combo‑treated cells implanted into immunocompetent mice show delayed growth and complete regression with anti‑PD‑1, unlike single‑agent controls.
In a clinical trial, patients who acquired both high TMB and MSI signatures after therapy had longer survival, though the primary endpoint was not met.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: p53‑mutant cell lines are less readily converted to MMR‑deficient phenotype.
Subclonal nature of induced neoantigens raises questions about durability of response.

combination_strategies.rationale: blocks checkpoint inhibition, allowing T cells to attack newly generated neoantigens
combination_strategies.phenotype: complete tumor regression in mouse models; improved OS in MSI‑converted patients

combination_strategies.rationale: platinum‑induced DNA cross‑links synergize with temozolomide‑mediated MSH2 silencing to accelerate MSI conversion
combination_strategies.phenotype: rapid TMB and MSI increase in vitro and in vivo

open_questions: Why do certain tumor genotypes (e.g., p53‑mutant) resist conversion to MMR deficiency?
Can alternative platinum agents (carboplatin, oxaliplatin) achieve similar MSH2 down‑regulation with better tolerability?
